categories.name: Python Object Base
categories.desc: Attributes found in almost all Python objects.

blocks.name: __class__
blocks.desc: Reference to the object's class.
point: Lets you inspect the runtime type of an object.
Usually equivalent to calling type(obj).

examples.code: type(5)

blocks.name: __dict__
blocks.desc: Dictionary storing an object's writable attributes.
point: Contains the object's attribute namespace.
Not present on objects using __slots__.
Often used for debugging or dynamic attribute manipulation.

examples.code:
class A:
    pass

a = A()
a.x = 10
a.__dict__

blocks.name: __doc__
blocks.desc: The documentation string associated with an object.
point: Used by help() and many documentation tools.
Usually defined as the first string literal in a class or function.

blocks.name: __text_signature__
blocks.desc: Text representation of a callable's signature.
point: Used by the inspect module.
Common on built-in functions implemented in C.

blocks.name: __module__
blocks.desc: Name of the module in which a class or function was defined.
point: Useful for reflection and debugging.
Helps identify where a class or object originates.

examples.code:
import math
math.sin.__module__

blocks.name: __annotations__
blocks.desc: Dictionary containing type annotations declared on the object.
point: Used heavily by static type checkers and frameworks.
Keys are variable or parameter names, values are type hints.

blocks.name: __dir__(...)
blocks.desc: Returns the list of attributes available on the object.
point: Called by the builtin dir() function.
Allows objects to customize their interactive attribute listing.
Typically returns a sorted list of attribute names.

blocks.name: __weakref__
blocks.desc: Slot used to store weak references to the object.
point: Automatically added for objects that support weak references.
Missing when __slots__ disables it.

examples.code:
import weakref
class A: pass
a = A()
weakref.ref(a)

blocks.name: __slots__
blocks.desc: Declares a fixed set of attributes to avoid using __dict__.
point: Reduces memory usage for many instances.
Prevents creation of arbitrary attributes.
May remove __dict__ and __weakref__ unless explicitly included.

examples.code:
class A:
    __slots__ = ('x',)

a = A()
a.x = 5

categories.name: Class Basics / Construction
categories.desc: Mechanisms controlling how classes create instances and how subclasses are initialized.
point: These methods control instance creation and class behaviour.
They are executed during object construction or subclass definition.
They form the foundation of Python's class system.

blocks.name: __new__(...)
blocks.desc: Creates and returns a new instance of the class.
point: Executed before __init__.
Responsible for allocating the instance.
Commonly overridden for immutable types.

blocks.name: __init__(...)
blocks.desc: Initializes a newly created instance.
point: Called immediately after __new__.
Should configure the object's state.
Must return None.

blocks.name: __init_subclass__(...)
blocks.desc: Customize class creation when a class is subclassed.
point: Called automatically when a class is subclassed.
Allows base classes to configure or validate subclasses.

blocks.name: __mro_entries__(...)
blocks.desc: Customize how objects in a class inheritance list affect the final MRO.
point: Used during class creation when resolving base classes.

subcategories.name: Metaclass Construction
subcategories.desc: Hooks controlling how class namespaces are created during class definition.

blocks.name: __prepare__(...)
blocks.desc: Create the namespace used to execute the class body.
point: Defined on metaclasses.
Executed before the class body runs.
Returns the mapping used as the class namespace.
Often used to control attribute order or custom class dictionaries.

examples.result: prints 'creating namespace' before class body executes

blocks.name: metaclass=Meta
blocks.desc: Specify a metaclass controlling class creation.
point: The metaclass defines how the class object is constructed.

blocks.name: __class_getitem__(...)
blocks.desc: Handle subscription of a class.
point: Called when a class is indexed using square brackets.
Used to implement generic types.

blocks.name: Class[T]
blocks.desc: Subscribe a class using square brackets.
point: Calls Class.__class_getitem__(T).

blocks.name: __bases__
blocks.desc: Tuple containing the direct base classes of a class.

blocks.name: __base__
blocks.desc: Immediate base class used for memory layout.
point: Usually the first entry of __bases__.
Represents the primary base class used internally by CPython.

blocks.name: __mro__
blocks.desc: Method Resolution Order for the class.
point: Defines the order Python searches for attributes.

blocks.name: __type_params__
blocks.desc: Type parameters declared on a generic class.
point: Introduced by PEP 695.
Used for new-style Python generics.

blocks.name: type(...)
blocks.desc: Return the type of an object.
point: Used to inspect the runtime type of objects.
Also acts as the metaclass for most Python classes.

categories.name: Type Checking Protocol
categories.desc: Hooks allowing classes or metaclasses to customize isinstance and issubclass behavior.
point: These methods are implemented on the metaclass.
They control how Python determines type relationships.
Used heavily by the abc module.

blocks.name: __instancecheck__(...)
blocks.desc: Customize the behavior of isinstance checks.
point: Called by isinstance(obj, Class).
Implemented on the metaclass.

blocks.name: __subclasscheck__(...)
blocks.desc: Customize the behavior of issubclass checks.
point: Called by issubclass(Sub, Class).
Implemented on the metaclass.

examples.result: classes defining run() count as subclasses

blocks.name: isinstance(...)
blocks.desc: Check whether an object is an instance of a class.
point: Calls Class.__instancecheck__(obj).

blocks.name: issubclass(...)
blocks.desc: Check whether a class is a subclass of another.
point: Calls Class.__subclasscheck__(Sub).

categories.name: Attribute Access Protocol
categories.desc: Mechanisms controlling how attributes are retrieved, assigned, or deleted on objects.
point: These methods define the attribute access protocol.
They are invoked by Python during attribute lookup or modification.
Built-in functions such as getattr and setattr use this protocol internally.

blocks.name: __getattribute__(...)
blocks.desc: Called for every attribute access on the instance.
point: Intercepts all obj.attr lookups.
Executed before __getattr__.
Must delegate to super().__getattribute__ to avoid infinite recursion.

blocks.name: __getattr__(...)
blocks.desc: Fallback method called when attribute lookup fails.
point: Called only if normal attribute lookup raises AttributeError.
Often used for lazy attributes or proxy objects.

blocks.name: __setattr__(...)
blocks.desc: Called when an attribute assignment occurs.
point: Intercepts obj.attr = value.
Custom implementations must call super().__setattr__ to perform the actual assignment.

blocks.name: __delattr__(...)
blocks.desc: Called when an attribute deletion occurs.
point: Intercepts del obj.attr.
Custom implementations must call super().__delattr__ to delete the attribute.

examples.code:
class A:
    x = 10

a = A()
del a.x

blocks.name: getattr(...)
blocks.desc: Retrieve an attribute value from an object.
point: Triggers the attribute access protocol.
Equivalent to obj.attr lookup.
Supports a default value if the attribute is missing.

blocks.name: setattr(...)
blocks.desc: Set an attribute on an object.
point: Triggers __setattr__ if defined.
Equivalent to obj.attr = value.

examples.code:
class A:
    pass

a = A()
setattr(a, 'x', 10)
a.x

blocks.name: delattr(...)
blocks.desc: Delete an attribute from an object.
point: Triggers __delattr__ if implemented.
Equivalent to del obj.attr.

examples.code:
class A:
    x = 10

a = A()
delattr(a, 'x')

blocks.name: hasattr(...)
blocks.desc: Check whether an object appears to have a given attribute.
point: Returns True if attribute lookup succeeds.
Internally implemented using getattr and catching AttributeError.
Triggers the full attribute access protocol.

categories.name: Import System Protocol
categories.desc: Attributes used by Python's module import system to describe how modules are located, loaded, and executed.
point: These attributes are automatically assigned to modules during import.
They provide metadata about the module's origin and loading process.
Used internally by Python's importlib machinery.

blocks.name: __loader__
blocks.desc: Object responsible for loading the module.
point: Part of Python's importlib system.
Provides the logic used to load the module.

blocks.name: __spec__
blocks.desc: Module specification describing how the module should be loaded.
point: Contains metadata about the module.
Created by the import system during module loading.

blocks.name: __package__
blocks.desc: Name of the package the module belongs to.
point: Used for resolving relative imports.

blocks.name: __file__
blocks.desc: Path to the module's source file.
point: Usually points to the .py file defining the module.
May point to compiled or bundled files depending on the loader.

examples.code:
import math
math.__file__

blocks.name: __cached__
blocks.desc: Path to the compiled bytecode cache file.
point: Usually points to a .pyc file in the __pycache__ directory.

blocks.name: __path__
blocks.desc: Search locations for submodules of a package.
point: Only present on packages, not normal modules.
Used by the import system to locate submodules.

categories.name: Memory / Object Lifecycle
categories.desc: Mechanisms related to the lifetime and destruction of objects.
point: These hooks control what happens when an object is about to be destroyed.
They interact with Python's garbage collector.
They should generally be avoided unless strictly necessary.

blocks.name: __del__(...)
blocks.desc: Called when an object is about to be destroyed.
point: Acts as a finalizer.
Triggered when the object is garbage collected.
Execution timing is not guaranteed.

blocks.name: del
blocks.desc: Remove a reference to an object.
point: Deletes a variable binding.
Does not necessarily destroy the object.
The object is destroyed only when its reference count reaches zero.

blocks.name: gc module
blocks.desc: Interface to Python's garbage collector.
point: Provides control over cyclic garbage collection.
Useful for debugging memory behavior.

examples.code:
import gc
gc.collect()

categories.name: Memory / Data Movement
categories.desc: Mechanisms controlling how objects expose, copy, and report their memory.
point: These protocols define how Python objects interact with memory.
They control copying behavior, raw memory access, and memory introspection.
Many performance-sensitive libraries rely heavily on these mechanisms.

subcategories.name: Copy Protocol
subcategories.desc: Methods allowing objects to control how they are duplicated.
point: Used by the copy module.
Allows objects to customize shallow and deep copying.
Important for complex objects containing references.

blocks.name: __copy__(...)
blocks.desc: Define shallow copy behavior.
point: Called by copy.copy(obj).
Should return a new object with the same references.

blocks.name: __deepcopy__(...)
blocks.desc: Define deep copy behavior.
point: Called by copy.deepcopy(obj).
Receives a memo dictionary tracking already-copied objects.
Should recursively copy referenced objects.

subcategories.name: Buffer Protocol
subcategories.desc: Mechanism allowing objects to expose raw memory buffers to other objects.
point: Allows zero-copy sharing of binary data.
Implemented primarily in CPython's C API.
Used heavily by libraries like NumPy and PIL.

blocks.name: Buffer
blocks.desc: Expose raw memory of an object without copying it.
point: Allows direct access to internal binary data.
Enables efficient data exchange between libraries.
Often implemented by bytes-like objects.

subcategories.name: Memory Views
subcategories.desc: Interface for interacting with objects exposing the buffer protocol.
point: Provides a view over raw memory buffers.
Allows slicing and inspection without copying data.
Commonly used in high-performance data processing.

blocks.name: memoryview(...)
blocks.desc: Create a memory view of an object exposing the buffer protocol.
point: Access binary data without copying.
Supports slicing and structured views.
Works with many bytes-like objects.

examples.code:
b = bytearray(b'abc')
m = memoryview(b)
examples.result: memory view referencing the same underlying buffer

subcategories.name: Memory Introspection
subcategories.desc: Methods allowing inspection of an object's memory footprint and identity.
point: Used for debugging and performance analysis.
Provides insight into memory usage and object identity.

blocks.name: __sizeof__(...)
blocks.desc: Return the size of the object in bytes.
point: Called by sys.getsizeof().
Represents the memory footprint of the object.
May exclude referenced objects.

blocks.name: sys.getsizeof(...)
blocks.desc: Return the memory size of an object.
point: Calls obj.__sizeof__() internally.
May add interpreter overhead.

blocks.name: id(...)
blocks.desc: Return the identity of an object.
point: Unique identifier for the object during its lifetime.
Often corresponds to the memory address in CPython.

categories.name: Pattern Matching Protocol
categories.desc: Mechanisms allowing classes to define how they are matched in structural pattern matching.
point: Used by the match statement introduced in Python 3.10.
Allows objects to define how positional patterns map to attributes.

blocks.name: __match_args__
blocks.desc: Define the attribute names used for positional pattern matching.
point: Maps positional patterns to attribute names.
Commonly generated automatically by dataclasses.

examples.code:
class Point:
    __match_args__ = ('x', 'y')

p = Point()
match p:
    case Point(a, b):
        print(a, b)
examples.result: Matches using attributes x and y

blocks.name: match / case
blocks.desc: Perform structural pattern matching.
point: Uses __match_args__ when matching positional class patterns.

categories.name: Descriptor Protocol
categories.desc: Mechanism allowing objects stored on classes to control attribute access on instances.
point: Descriptors customize attribute access for class attributes.
They are heavily used by Python's object model.
Functions, properties, and methods are implemented using descriptors.

blocks.name: __get__(...)
blocks.value_type: Callable[[Self, object | None, type | None], Any]
blocks.desc: Retrieve the value of a descriptor.
point: Called when accessing a descriptor attribute.
instance is the instance accessing the attribute.
owner is the class where the descriptor is defined.

blocks.name: __set__(...)
blocks.desc: Set the value of a descriptor attribute.
point: Called when assigning to the descriptor.
Defines a data descriptor when present.

blocks.name: __delete__(...)
blocks.desc: Delete the descriptor value from an instance.
point: Called when deleting a descriptor attribute.
Used less frequently but completes the data descriptor interface.

blocks.name: __set_name__(...)
blocks.desc: Called when the descriptor is assigned to a class attribute.
point: Executed during class creation.
Allows the descriptor to know its attribute name.

blocks.name: property(...)
blocks.value_type: Callable[[Callable[[Self], Any] | None, Callable[[Self, Any], None] | None, Callable[[Self], None] | None], property]
blocks.desc: Create a managed attribute using the descriptor protocol.
point: Implements __get__, __set__, and __delete__ internally.
Commonly used to control attribute access.

blocks.name: classmethod(...)
blocks.desc: Convert a function into a class method.
point: Uses the descriptor protocol to bind the class instead of the instance.

blocks.name: staticmethod(...)
blocks.desc: Convert a function into a static method.
point: Uses the descriptor protocol but does not bind instance or class.

categories.name: Callable Protocol
categories.desc: Methods allowing objects to behave like functions.
point: Objects implementing __call__ can be invoked like functions.
The call syntax obj(...) is translated into obj.__call__(...).
Commonly used for functors, decorators, and callable classes.

blocks.name: __call__(...)
blocks.desc: Invoke the object as a callable.
point: Executed when the object is called like a function.
Receives the same arguments passed to the call.

blocks.name: obj(...)
blocks.desc: Call an object using function-call syntax.
point: Translated internally to obj.__call__(...).

blocks.name: callable(...)
blocks.desc: Check whether an object is callable.
point: Returns True if the object implements __call__ or is a callable type.

categories.name: Context Manager Protocol
categories.desc: Mechanisms allowing objects to manage resources using the with statement.
point: Context managers handle setup and cleanup of resources.
Used by the with statement.
Commonly used for files, locks, and temporary environments.

blocks.name: __enter__(...)
blocks.desc: Enter the runtime context.
point: Executed at the beginning of the with block.
Its return value is bound to the variable after 'as'.

blocks.name: __exit__(...)
blocks.value_type: Callable[[Self, type | None, BaseException | None, object | None], bool | None]
blocks.desc: Exit the runtime context.
point: Executed when leaving the with block.
Receives exception information if an error occurred.
Returning True suppresses the exception.

blocks.name: with obj as name
blocks.desc: Execute a block within a managed context.
point: Calls obj.__enter__() at the beginning.
Calls obj.__exit__() when leaving the block.

categories.name: Representation / Stringification
categories.desc: Methods controlling how objects convert to strings or other representations.
point: These methods define how objects are displayed or formatted.
__repr__ is intended for developers and debugging.
__str__ is intended for end-user readable output.

blocks.name: __repr__(...)
blocks.desc: Return the official string representation of the object.
point: Used for debugging and development.
Should ideally return a string that could recreate the object.
Fallback used by str() if __str__ is not defined.

blocks.name: __str__(...)
blocks.desc: Return a human-readable string representation.
point: Used by print() and str().
Intended for user-friendly display.
Falls back to __repr__ if not defined.

blocks.name: __format__(...)
blocks.desc: Customize formatting using the format specification mini-language.
point: Called by format().
Used by f-strings.
Receives the format specification string.

blocks.name: __bytes__(...)
blocks.desc: Return a byte-string representation of the object.
point: Called by the built-in bytes().
Used for binary representations.

blocks.name: repr(...)
blocks.desc: Return the canonical string representation of an object.
point: Calls obj.__repr__().

blocks.name: str(...)
blocks.desc: Return a readable string representation of an object.
point: Calls obj.__str__() if available.
Otherwise falls back to __repr__.

blocks.name: format(...)
blocks.desc: Format an object using the format specification mini-language.
point: Calls obj.__format__(spec).

categories.name: Hashing
categories.desc: Mechanisms allowing objects to be used in hash-based collections.
point: Hash values are used by dictionaries and sets.
Objects that compare equal must produce the same hash.

blocks.name: __hash__(...)
blocks.desc: Return the hash value of the object.
point: Called by hash(obj).
Must remain constant during the object's lifetime.

blocks.name: hash(...)
blocks.desc: Return the hash value of an object.
point: Calls obj.__hash__().

categories.name: Container / Collection Protocol
categories.desc: Methods allowing objects to behave like containers or collections.
point: These methods define how objects expose their elements.
They enable iteration, membership checks, indexing, and length queries.

blocks.name: __len__(...)
blocks.desc: Return the number of items in the container.
point: Must return a non-negative integer.
Used by many Python operations.

blocks.name: len(...)
blocks.desc: Return the number of elements in a container.
point: Calls obj.__len__().

blocks.name: __contains__(...)
blocks.desc: Check whether an element exists in the container.
point: Implements the membership test operator.
Should return True or False.

blocks.name: a in b
blocks.desc: Test membership in a container.
point: Calls b.__contains__(a).
If missing, Python falls back to iteration.

blocks.name: __getitem__(...)
blocks.desc: Retrieve an element using an index or key.
point: Used for indexing operations.
Often implemented by sequences and mappings.

blocks.name: container[key]
blocks.desc: Access an element using indexing syntax.
point: Calls container.__getitem__(key).

blocks.name: __setitem__(...)
blocks.desc: Assign a value to an index or key.
point: Implements indexed assignment.
Commonly used by mutable sequences and mappings.

blocks.name: container[key] = value
blocks.desc: Assign an element using indexing syntax.
point: Calls container.__setitem__(key, value).

blocks.name: __delitem__(...)
blocks.desc: Delete an element from the container.
point: Implements deletion of indexed or keyed elements.

blocks.name: del container[key]
blocks.desc: Delete an element using indexing syntax.
point: Calls container.__delitem__(key).

subcategories.name: Integer Indexing
subcategories.desc: Protocol allowing objects to behave like integers in indexing contexts.

blocks.name: __index__(...)
blocks.desc: Return the integer representation of the object for indexing operations.
point: Used when Python requires an exact integer.
Different from __int__ which performs numeric conversion.
Commonly implemented by integer-like objects.

blocks.name: container[obj]
blocks.desc: Use an object as an index.
point: Python calls obj.__index__() to obtain an integer.

blocks.name: slice indices
blocks.desc: Objects used in slice positions must implement __index__.

blocks.name: bin / hex / oct
blocks.desc: Convert an integer-like object to binary, hexadecimal, or octal.
point: Uses the __index__ method.

blocks.name: __bool__(...)
blocks.desc: Return the truth value of the object.
point: Called when Python evaluates the object in a boolean context.
If not implemented, Python falls back to __len__.

blocks.name: bool(...)
blocks.desc: Return the truth value of an object.
point: Calls obj.__bool__() if defined.
Otherwise falls back to __len__.

subcategories.name: Numeric Conversion
subcategories.desc: Methods allowing objects to convert themselves to numeric types.

blocks.name: __int__(...)
blocks.desc: Convert the object to an integer.
point: Called by int(obj).
Should return an integer representation of the object.

blocks.name: __float__(...)
blocks.desc: Convert the object to a floating-point number.
point: Called by float(obj).
Should return a float representation of the object.

blocks.name: __complex__(...)
blocks.desc: Convert the object to a complex number.
point: Called by complex(obj).

blocks.name: __round__(...)
blocks.desc: Customize rounding behavior.
point: Called by round(obj).
Receives the optional ndigits argument.

blocks.name: int(...)
blocks.desc: Convert a value to an integer.
point: Calls obj.__int__() if defined.

blocks.name: float(...)
blocks.desc: Convert a value to a floating-point number.
point: Calls obj.__float__() if defined.

blocks.name: complex(...)
blocks.desc: Convert a value to a complex number.
point: Calls obj.__complex__() if defined.

categories.name: Iterator Protocol
categories.desc: Mechanisms allowing objects to produce a sequence of values one at a time.
point: Iterators produce values sequentially.
They are typically returned by __iter__.
Iteration continues until StopIteration is raised.

blocks.name: __next__(...)
blocks.desc: Return the next item from the iterator.
point: Called repeatedly during iteration.
Must raise StopIteration when no items remain.

blocks.name: next(...)
blocks.desc: Retrieve the next item from an iterator.
point: Calls iterator.__next__().
If a default value is provided, it is returned instead of raising StopIteration.

blocks.name: __iter__(...)
blocks.desc: Return an iterator for the object.
point: Used by for-loops and many built-in functions.
The returned object must implement __next__.

blocks.name: for x in container
blocks.desc: Iterate over elements of a container.
point: Calls iter(container).
Then repeatedly calls next(...) on the iterator.

blocks.name: iter(...)
blocks.value_type: Callable[[object], Iterator[Any]] | Callable[[Callable[[], Any], Any], Iterator[Any]]
blocks.desc: Return an iterator for an object.
point: Calls obj.__iter__() when available.
If __iter__ is missing, Python falls back to the sequence protocol using __getitem__.
The two-argument form repeatedly calls a function until a sentinel value is returned.

blocks.name: __reversed__(...)
blocks.desc: Return an iterator yielding elements in reverse order.
point: Called by reversed(obj).
If missing, Python may fall back to __len__ and __getitem__.

blocks.name: reversed(...)
blocks.desc: Return a reverse iterator over a container.
point: Calls obj.__reversed__() if available.

blocks.name: __length_hint__(...)
blocks.desc: Return an estimate of the number of remaining elements.
point: Used as a performance optimization.
Commonly implemented by iterators.
The returned value may be approximate.

blocks.name: operator.length_hint(...)
blocks.desc: Return the length hint of an iterable.
point: Calls obj.__length_hint__() if available.
Returns 0 if no hint is provided.

categories.name: Generator Protocol
categories.desc: Mechanisms allowing functions to produce values lazily using yield.
point: Generators implement the iterator protocol automatically.
Each yield pauses execution and returns a value.
Execution resumes when the next value is requested.

blocks.name: yield
blocks.desc: Pause execution and produce a value from a generator.
point: Used inside generator functions.
Suspends execution and returns a value to the caller.

blocks.name: yield from
blocks.desc: Delegate iteration to another iterable.
point: Used to compose generators.
Passes values and control between generators.

blocks.name: send(...)
blocks.desc: Resume the generator and send a value into it.
point: The value becomes the result of the suspended yield expression.

blocks.name: throw(...)
blocks.value_type: Callable[[Self, type, BaseException | None, object | None], Any]
blocks.desc: Raise an exception inside the generator.
point: The exception is raised at the suspended yield.

blocks.name: close(...)
blocks.desc: Terminate the generator.
point: Raises GeneratorExit inside the generator.

categories.name: Async Protocols
categories.desc: Protocols used by Python's asynchronous execution model.
point: These methods define the behavior of awaitables, coroutines, and async iterators.
They power async/await syntax.

blocks.name: asyncio
blocks.desc: High-level asynchronous framework built on the async protocols.
point: Provides event loops, tasks, and scheduling.
Uses awaitables, async iterators, and async context managers.

blocks.name: __await__(...)
blocks.desc: Return an iterator used to implement await.
point: Called when awaiting an object.
Must return an iterator.

blocks.name: await obj
blocks.desc: Suspend execution until the awaitable completes.
point: Calls obj.__await__().

blocks.name: __aiter__(...)
blocks.desc: Return an asynchronous iterator.

blocks.name: __anext__(...)
blocks.desc: Return the next item asynchronously.

blocks.name: async for
blocks.desc: Iterate asynchronously over an async iterator.
point: Calls __aiter__ and repeatedly awaits __anext__.

blocks.name: __aenter__(...)
blocks.desc: Async version of __enter__.

blocks.name: __aexit__(...)
blocks.value_type: Callable[[Self, type | None, BaseException | None, object | None], Awaitable]
blocks.desc: Async version of __exit__.

blocks.name: async with
blocks.desc: Execute a block within an asynchronous context manager.

blocks.name: async def ... yield
blocks.desc: Define an asynchronous generator.
point: Produces values asynchronously.
Used with async for.

blocks.name: anext(...)
blocks.desc: Retrieve the next item from an async iterator.

categories.name: Comparison Protocol
categories.desc: Methods controlling comparison behavior between objects.
point: These methods implement Python's rich comparison protocol.
Each comparison operator maps to a specific dunder method.
Methods should return True, False, or NotImplemented.
If NotImplemented is returned, Python may attempt the reflected comparison.

blocks.name: __eq__(...)
blocks.desc: Equality comparison.
point: Implements the == operator.
Should return NotImplemented for unsupported types.

blocks.name: __ne__(...)
blocks.desc: Inequality comparison.
point: Implements the != operator.
By default Python uses the inverse of __eq__ if not defined.

blocks.name: __lt__(...)
blocks.desc: Less-than comparison.
point: Implements the < operator.

blocks.name: __le__(...)
blocks.desc: Less-than-or-equal comparison.
point: Implements the <= operator.

blocks.name: __gt__(...)
blocks.desc: Greater-than comparison.
point: Implements the > operator.

blocks.name: __ge__(...)
blocks.desc: Greater-than-or-equal comparison.
point: Implements the >= operator.

blocks.name: functools.total_ordering(...)
blocks.desc: Generate missing comparison methods automatically.
point: Requires at least __eq__ and one ordering method.
Automatically fills the remaining comparison methods.

blocks.name: NotImplemented
blocks.desc: Special return value indicating that an operation is not implemented for the given operands.
point: Used by binary operator methods such as __eq__, __add__, etc.
Signals to Python that the operation should be attempted using the reflected method.
Should be returned instead of raising an exception when an operation is unsupported.

examples.result: allows Python to try the reflected comparison

categories.name: Numeric / Operator Protocol
categories.desc: Methods allowing objects to emulate numeric and operator behavior.
point: Python operators are implemented through special methods.
Binary operators often exist in three forms: normal, reflected, and in-place.
Unary operators use a separate protocol.

subcategories.name: Operator Patterns
subcategories.desc: General behavior shared by binary operators.

blocks.name: Binary Operator Pattern
blocks.desc: Standard implementation of a binary operator.
point: Called when the object appears on the left side of the operator.
Should return NotImplemented when the other operand type is unsupported.

blocks.name: Reflected Operator Pattern
blocks.desc: Fallback operator when the left operand does not support the operation.
point: Called when the left operand returns NotImplemented.
Allows the right operand to implement the operation.

blocks.name: In-place Operator Pattern
blocks.desc: Operator used for augmented assignment.
point: Called by operators such as += or *=.
May mutate the object in place.
If not implemented, Python falls back to normal operator behavior.

blocks.name: __add__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement the + operator.

blocks.name: __radd__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement reflected +.

blocks.name: __iadd__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement in-place +.

blocks.name: __sub__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement the - operator.

blocks.name: __rsub__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement reflected -.

blocks.name: __isub__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement in-place -.

blocks.name: __mul__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement the * operator.

blocks.name: __rmul__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement reflected *.

blocks.name: __imul__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement in-place *.

blocks.name: __matmul__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement the @ operator.

blocks.name: __rmatmul__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement reflected @.

blocks.name: __imatmul__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement in-place @.

blocks.name: __truediv__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement the / operator.

blocks.name: __rtruediv__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement reflected /.

blocks.name: __itruediv__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement in-place /.

blocks.name: __floordiv__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement the // operator.

blocks.name: __rfloordiv__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement reflected //.

blocks.name: __ifloordiv__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement in-place //.

blocks.name: __mod__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement the % operator.

blocks.name: __rmod__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement reflected %.

blocks.name: __imod__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement in-place %.

blocks.name: __pow__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement the ** operator.

blocks.name: __rpow__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement reflected **.

blocks.name: __ipow__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement in-place **.

blocks.name: __divmod__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement divmod(a, b).

blocks.name: __rdivmod__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement reflected divmod.

blocks.name: __and__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement the & operator.

blocks.name: __rand__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement reflected &.

blocks.name: __iand__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement in-place &.

blocks.name: __or__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement the | operator.

blocks.name: __ror__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement reflected |.

blocks.name: __ior__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement in-place |.

blocks.name: __xor__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement the ^ operator.

blocks.name: __rxor__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement reflected ^.

blocks.name: __ixor__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement in-place ^.

blocks.name: __lshift__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement the << operator.

blocks.name: __rlshift__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement reflected <<.

blocks.name: __ilshift__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement in-place <<.

blocks.name: __rshift__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement the >> operator.

blocks.name: __rrshift__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement reflected >>.

blocks.name: __irshift__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement in-place >>.

blocks.name: __neg__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement unary negation.

blocks.name: __pos__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement unary positive.

blocks.name: __abs__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement unary absolute.

blocks.name: __invert__(...)
blocks.desc: Implement unary bitwise invert.

categories.name: Path Protocol
categories.desc: Protocol allowing objects to represent filesystem paths.
point: Defines how objects convert to filesystem paths.
Used by many standard library functions.
Implemented by pathlib.Path and custom path-like objects.

blocks.name: __fspath__(...)
blocks.desc: Return the filesystem path representation of the object.
point: Called by os.fspath().
Should return a string or bytes path.

blocks.name: os.fspath(...)
blocks.desc: Return the filesystem path representation of a path-like object.
point: Calls obj.__fspath__() if implemented.
Returns the object unchanged if it is already str or bytes.

blocks.name: os.PathLike
blocks.desc: Abstract base class for objects implementing the path protocol.
point: Defines the __fspath__ method.
Used for type checking.

blocks.name: pathlib.Path(...)
blocks.desc: Object-oriented representation of filesystem paths.
point: Implements the Path Protocol.
Provides convenient filesystem operations.

examples.code:
from pathlib import Path
p = Path('file.txt')
open(p)
examples.result: Path works anywhere a path is expected

categories.name: Abstract Base Class Protocol
categories.desc: Mechanisms allowing classes to customize subclass checks.
point: Used by the abc module.
Allows classes to define virtual subclass relationships.

blocks.name: __subclasshook__(...)
blocks.desc: Customize the behavior of issubclass checks.
point: Called by issubclass().
Allows classes to declare virtual subclasses.

examples.result: classes implementing run() count as subclasses

blocks.name: issubclass(...)
blocks.desc: Check whether a class is considered a subclass of another.
point: Uses the subclass hook if defined.

categories.name: Serialization Protocols
categories.desc: Methods allowing objects to define how they are serialized, copied, and reconstructed.
point: Used by pickle, copy, multiprocessing, and many libraries.
Allow objects to control how their state is saved and restored.

blocks.name: __reduce__(...)
blocks.desc: Return instructions for reconstructing the object during pickling.
point: Low-level pickle protocol hook.
Returns a tuple describing how to rebuild the object.

blocks.name: __reduce_ex__(...)
blocks.desc: Version-aware variant of __reduce__.
point: Receives the pickle protocol version.
Preferred by the pickle module.

blocks.name: __getnewargs__(...)
blocks.desc: Return arguments for the object's __new__ method during unpickling.
point: Used for objects requiring arguments during construction.
Returns a tuple passed to __new__.

blocks.name: __getnewargs_ex__(...)
blocks.desc: Return positional and keyword arguments for __new__ during unpickling.
point: More flexible version of __getnewargs__.
Returns (args, kwargs).

blocks.name: __getstate__(...)
blocks.desc: Return the serializable state of the object.
point: Used by pickle to extract object state.
Often returns a dictionary.

blocks.name: __setstate__(...)
blocks.desc: Restore object state during unpickling.
point: Receives the state returned by __getstate__.

blocks.name: pickle.dumps(...)
blocks.desc: Serialize an object to a byte string.

blocks.name: pickle.loads(...)
blocks.desc: Deserialize an object from a byte string.

categories.name: CPython Implementation Details
categories.desc: Attributes exposing the internal memory layout of CPython types.
point: These attributes describe the internal structure of Python objects in CPython.
They are mainly useful for interpreter developers and advanced introspection.
They are implementation details and may not exist in other Python implementations.

blocks.name: __basicsize__
blocks.desc: Size of the base object structure in bytes.
point: Represents the fixed memory size of instances of the type.
Used internally by CPython when allocating objects.

blocks.name: __itemsize__
blocks.desc: Size of each additional element for variable-sized objects.
point: Used for types that store extra elements after the base structure.
Examples include tuples and other variable-length objects.

blocks.name: __dictoffset__
blocks.desc: Offset of the instance dictionary within the object structure.
point: Defines where the instance __dict__ is stored.
Used internally by CPython's object layout.

blocks.name: __weakrefoffset__
blocks.desc: Offset of the weak reference list within the object structure.
point: Used internally by CPython to support weak references.

blocks.name: __flags__
blocks.desc: Bit flags describing interpreter-level properties of the type.
point: Used internally by CPython's runtime.
Defines characteristics such as heap allocation or garbage collection support.
